hero.subtitle: AI and embedded systems that survive contact with real hardware — validated on the chip, not just in simulation.

proof.stat1_label: Faster inference, measured on real ESP32 hardware
proof.stat3_label: Less energy than streaming to the cloud
proof.affiliations: IEEE · Tecnológico de Monterrey · Universidad Iberoamericana Puebla · Universidad Veracruzana

about.title: Profile
about.p1: I am Alessandro Reyes, a Mechatronics Engineering student at IBERO Puebla. My work sits at the intersection of robust mechanical design, embedded systems, and applied artificial intelligence.
about.p2: From deploying machine learning models on microcontrollers to architecting closed-loop control systems for competitive robotics, I build technology that works in the real world, not just in simulation.
about.availability: Open to Summer 2026 internships & international research collaborations

projects.larc_impact: Built and raced a custom ESP32-C6 robot that recovers from 90° turns in milliseconds — engineered and debugged under real competition pressure at LARC 2025.
projects.case_title: Edge AI Decision Framework: Quantifying the Sensitivity-Latency Trade-off
projects.case_impact: Found a hardware bug that silently broke a 'perfect' ML model, then built a model 126× faster with zero false alarms — validated on real ESP32 silicon, not simulation.
projects.bdai_title: Industrial Fault Detection via Machine Learning
projects.bdai_impact: 99.85% accuracy, zero false positives, 98.4% less energy than the cloud — predictive maintenance light enough to run on a $5 microcontroller.
projects.balancin_impact: Real-time computer vision and inverse kinematics keep a rolling ball balanced on a moving plate — and keep predicting its trajectory even when a camera frame drops.

larc.title: LARC 2025 at Tec de Monterrey.
larc.subtitle: The Tracky Project.
larc.description: Building hardware is one thing; making it survive a chaotic, high-stakes arena is another. At the 25th Latin American Robotics Competition (LARC) in Nuevo León, we put our engineering to the ultimate test. Facing off against top universities across Latin America in the TIR Line Follower category, we deployed our custom ESP32-C6 robot, Tracky, into a fiercely contested race where every millisecond mattered.
larc.venue_placeholder_desc: Drop your "PXL..." arena photo into public/images/ and rename it to "larc_arena.jpg"
larc.section1_p1: LARC 2025 wasn't just a showcase; it was an absolute proving ground. Held at the Tecnológico de Monterrey in Nuevo León, the environment was intense—filled with the smell of fresh solder, the high-pitch whine of racing servos, and teams pushing their hardware to the breaking point.
larc.section1_p2: As a two-person team, agility was our best weapon. We were debugging C++ memory leaks on the fly, fighting BLE interference, and constantly recalibrating the IR arrays to match the arena's changing ambient light. It was real engineering under fire—where theoretical math meets physical reality, and your code either holds the line or crashes out.
larc.section2_desc: Tracky is built on low inertia and extreme processing speed. We ditched the standard Arduino loops and leveraged the ESP32-C6 (RISC-V core), implementing an RTOS-like architecture that guarantees our control loop fires instantly, making the robot hunt the line instead of just following it.
larc.card1_desc: Most line followers fail catastrophically on sharp 90° bends. To prevent this, we coded a Pure Inertial Recovery protocol. If all 8 IR sensors hit white, Tracky instantly pulls the derivative vector of its last known position and spins aggressively in that exact direction until it catches the track again.
larc.card2_desc: Reflashing code between heats is a rookie mistake. We wired physical DIP switches on the chassis to hot-swap between pre-calibrated PID profiles: Slow Test (Purple), Balanced (Blue), Aggressive (Red), and a High-Damping Mode (Green) specifically tuned for chaotic zig-zag sections.
larc.card3_desc: We developed a real-time Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) dashboard in Python serving as a wireless "Optical Viewfinder" to tune PID parameters live. If the connection dropped due to arena interference, Tracky's architecture featured an Auto-Healing routine—ignoring the drop and continuing the race autonomously.
larc.hw1: Swapped out inferior drivers to handle high-current burst maneuvers, ensuring the motors received clean PWM signals.
larc.hw2: Orchestrated a multiplexing circuit to poll all 8 phototransistors at lighting speed, feeding the PID error calculator with millimeter-perfect coordinates.
larc.hw3: Served as the core brain, running concurrent RTOS-like logic to manage BLE telemetry, sensor polling, and kinematic math without blocking the main loop.

balancin.title: Self-Balancing Platform with Computer Vision.
balancin.description: A mechatronic system designed to continuously balance a rubber ball on a flat plane. By integrating Python-based OpenCV computer vision with an ESP32 microcontroller, the system calculates the trajectory of the ball and actuates three servomotors in real-time to maintain equilibrium.
balancin.section1_p1: The primary goal of this project was to successfully balance a dynamic object (a rubber ball) on a flat, moving surface. This required a multidisciplinary approach encompassing mechanical design for stability, electronic integration, and complex control algorithms to ensure rapid and accurate responsiveness.
balancin.section1_p2: We utilized computer-aided design (CATIA V5) to model the articulators and 3D printing (PLA) alongside laser-cut MDF to construct the physical base, verifying that the physical tolerances could handle rapid servo actuation without structural failure.
balancin.section2_p1: The intelligence of the system is split into two main components: a Python-based Vision & Control Hub, and an ESP32 embedded execution unit.
balancin.li1_desc: Using a camera and OpenCV, the system tracks a dynamic "blob" representing the ball across the color spectrum (HSV isolation). It simultaneously detects the bounds of the MDF plate to calculate relative positioning coordinates.
balancin.li2_desc: A Proportional-Integral-Derivative equation computes the exact spatial error and derivative speed to anticipate where the ball is heading, generating correction vectors to slow it down before it falls edge-side.
balancin.li3_desc: The correction vectors are sent over Bluetooth Serial to the ESP32. The microcontroller runs a C++ script that maps the 2D Cartesian plane corrections into a 3D inverse kinematic model, translating the values into pulse widths that command the three distinct MG996R servomotors to tilt the plane.
balancin.section3_p1: By combining high-torque servos with an aggressively tuned PID controller, the platform demonstrates stable, sustained equilibrium. The memory-vector system implemented in the OpenCV pipeline ensures that even if a frame drops or lighting changes, the platform predicts the last known trajectory, providing incredible robustness against physical disturbances.

bdai.title: Comparative Evaluation of Lightweight Supervised Machine Learning Techniques for Industrial Rotating Machinery.
bdai.conference_val: IEEE 9th International Conference on Big Data and Artificial Intelligence (BDAI 2026)
bdai.abstract_text: This study evaluates the performance of three supervised machine learning algorithms—Random Forest (RF), Logistic Regression (LR), and Support Vector Machine (SVM)—in detecting bearing faults at different degradation stages using the NASA IMS dataset. The methodology involves computing statistical features (RMS, Peak, Crest Factor, Kurtosis) from vibration signals and employing the Fisher Discriminant Ratio (FDR) to separate the data into Incipient and Advanced fault stages.
bdai.index_terms_val: Predictive Maintenance, NASA IMS Dataset, Machine Learning, Random Forest, Logistic Regression, FDR.
bdai.fig1: Algorithm Comparison. Performance vs Latency trade-off visualization.
bdai.section1_p1: The project follows a rigorous data science pipeline focusing on extracting time-domain statistical features from raw vibration data (984 files from NASA IMS Bearing 1).
bdai.section1_p2: Using the Fisher Discriminant Ratio to mathematically define when a bearing transitions from 'Healthy' to 'Incipient Fault', and finally to 'Advanced Fault'.
bdai.fig2: Fig. 2. Time-domain vibration analysis: Contrast between a healthy operational state and a critical fault.
bdai.fig3: Fig. 3. Importance analysis: RMS value dominates as the most discriminative metric for fault detection compared to variables like Skewness or Shape Factor.
bdai.section2_p1: To ensure the model was lightweight enough for a microcontroller, the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) was deliberately omitted, as it requires logarithmic complexity O(N log N). Instead, the system evaluates the one-dimensional statistical temporal evolution of vibrations at O(N) complexity.
bdai.section2_p2: Candidate metrics such as Shape Factor, Crest Factor, Skewness, and Kurtosis were computed. After Variable Importance analysis (Gini impurity), it was concluded that Root Mean Square (RMS) and peak amplitude magnitude carried the highest weight in the classifier, making them ideal features due to their monotonic trend directly correlated to progressive physical wear.
bdai.section3_p1: To prevent overfitting, hyperparameters were tuned via structured cross-validation over temporal cycles to isolate data leakage. The SVM and k-NN models proved competitive, but the Random Forest (100 estimators) dominated with over 99% effectiveness. At the analytical level, reporting 0 false positives was the fundamental milestone for validating it as a safe emergency halt protocol without arbitrarily stopping the plant.
bdai.edge_desc: This approach was mathematically extrapolated to an IoT-connected architecture (ESP32-S3 with an RTOS). By inferring the tree branches locally on hardware and using MQTT to transmit exclusively an alarm payload, the device is theoretically estimated to consume 0.99 mJ per execution — a 98% energy saving compared to streaming all raw data over Wi-Fi to the Cloud.
bdai.fig4: Fig. 4. Optimized model confusion matrix, demonstrating complete reliability with zero false positives or false negatives (False Acceptance Rate = 0%).
bdai.cta_desc: Accepted to IEEE BDAI 2026; camera-ready manuscript submitted and registration fees paid. Proceedings will be published via IEEE Xplore and indexed in Ei Compendex and Scopus following the conference (July 2026).
bdai.cta_btn: View on IEEE Xplore (Available After Conference)

case.title: Edge AI Decision Framework: Quantifying the Sensitivity-Latency Trade-off in Industrial Bearing Predictive Maintenance.
case.conference_val: 22nd IEEE International Conference on Automation Science and Engineering (CASE 2026)
case.abstract_text: There are several classification strategies available for evaluating when a bearing has failed. Bearing failure is a gradual process, and the question posed is whether or not the best classifier should also be allowed to change based on the fault’s progression. Using measurements which come from the NASA Intelligent Maintenance Systems (IMS) 2nd_test dataset (984 total files that measure Bearing 1, which has a fault on the outer race), three classifiers—Random Forest (RF), Logistic Regression (LR), and Support Vector Machine (SVM)—were evaluated with respect to their ability to detect faults at two stages of degradation. At the incipient stage, RF detected 27 out of 56 fault cycles (48.2% detection rate) while LR detected only 7 out of 56 (12.5%), representing a difference of 3.9×. At the advanced stage, both classifiers converged to Area Under the Curve (AUC) = 1.000. Latency was measured using CPU cycle-counting directly from a real-world ESP32 microcontroller (Xtensa LX6, 240 MHz), not from simulation. LR had a latency of 184 ns while RF had a latency of 23.3 μs, i.e., a 126× difference. SVM misclassified the fault on hardware at 96.5 μs and was disqualified. Fisher Discriminant Ratio (FDR) analysis shows a 5.89× separability increase from incipient to advanced stage, and a label-free proxy trigger validated on IMS Bearing 2 yielded zero false positives. To the best of the authors’ knowledge, no prior study has characterized this trade-off based on degradation stage using physical microcontroller hardware.
case.index_terms_val: Edge AI, Predictive Maintenance, TinyML, Bearing Fault Detection, Fisher Discriminant Ratio, Hardware Benchmark.
case.fig1: Conceptual Diagram. Proposed deployment architecture: Adaptive inference loop on ESP32 for multi-stage bearing fault detection.
case.li1_desc: Unlike prior simulations, this model was deployed and evaluated on a physical ESP32 using the EloquentTinyML library.
case.li2_desc: An FDR-based method with statistical features (RMS, Kurtosis, Peak) segments bearing health into three states: Healthy, Incipient, and Advanced.
case.li3_desc: Inference time was measured directly on the microcontroller. A lightweight Random Forest takes 23.3 μs (5,592 CPU cycles) per inference, while optimized Logistic Regression requires only 184 ns (44 cycles), enabling ultra-efficient real-time monitoring.
case.li4_desc: The system uses an adaptive scheme: a heavier model (Random Forest) operates in the healthy state, then switches to an ultra-lightweight model (Logistic Regression) once a fault is detected, maximizing energy efficiency.
case.fig2: Fig 1. Statistical feature evolution and degradation stage segmentation based on the FDR framework.
case.section2_p1: Through the Fisher Discriminant Ratio (FDR) metric, the system mathematically distinguishes when a defect is minor (Incipient) versus when it shows considerable mechanical degradation (Advanced).
case.section2_p2: Upon crossing this threshold, the system dynamically stops running the Random Forest (a moderate battery consumer) and switches exclusively to Logistic Regression to continue tracking the advanced fault at a much lower energy cost.
case.section3_p1: Demonstrating software improvements on PC using emulation is relatively trivial. In this work, we iterated by directly measuring CPU clock usage on the real Xtensa LX6 architecture (ESP32).
case.section3_box: We avoided tools like micros() that inject overhead in C++, and instead read the native hardware cycle-counter registers directly to ensure nanosecond precision — mathematically validating why Logistic Regression represents the optimal choice for passively powered remote IoT.
case.fig3: Fig 2. Hardware latency benchmark: Logistic Regression achieves 126× faster inference than Random Forest on the ESP32.
case.fig4: Fig 3. PCA analysis illustrating fault progression from Incipient to Advanced degradation stage.
case.section4_p1: The system was validated on an independent monitoring channel (IMS Bearing 2) to ensure performance under normal operating conditions, maintaining a record of zero false alarms and achieving specificity above 98%.
case.edge_desc: During physical testing, the SVM model was disqualified due to catastrophic floating-point precision errors on the ESP32 FPU, where precision loss in the expf() instruction inverted valid decision boundaries. This underscores the critical urgency of validating benchmarks on the final hardware rather than relying solely on analytical simulations.
case.cta_desc: Accepted to IEEE CASE 2026; camera-ready manuscript submitted and registration fees paid. The author will present remotely; proceedings will be published via IEEE Xplore following the conference.
